metadata.title: Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews

abstract:
Employee reviews on platforms like Glassdoor and Indeed significantly influence organizational attractiveness of millions of prospective applicants. To deepen our understanding of this phenomenon, we examined the effects of employee review characteristics on perceived helpfulnessa proximal indicator of adopting shared information. Specifically, we investigated the relationship between the sentiment of organic Glassdoor reviews (ranging from positive to negative attitudes) and their helpfulness ratings. Additionally, we explored the moderating roles of overall corporate ratings and employee status in shaping the impact of employee reviews.

Employing automated text analysis with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) and Structural Topic Modeling, we further delved into employee review content to extract the topics discussed and how their attributes (e.g., the extent to which the topic is discussed) influence perceived helpfulness. Drawing insights from an extensive analysis of 24,687 Glassdoor reviews, our findings revealed that negative reviews of lower rated organizations tend to receive higher helpfulness ratings, particularly when provided by former employees. The topics identified through LDA encompassed both instrumental and symbolic aspects of organizations, with their extent of discussion uniquely interacting with sentiment. Our study sheds light on the profound impact of employee satisfaction on the perceived helpfulness of online reviews. By presenting a comprehensive analysis of online reviews, this research offers valuable insights for businesses to enhance their organizational attractiveness and better understand the dynamics of online reputation management.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Researchers have long been invested in uncovering stakeholders' (e. g., consumers, applicants, employees and investors) attitudes toward organizations as well as identifying the mechanisms through which these attitudes develop (Cable & Turban, 2003;Chapman et al., 2005;Rynes & Barber, 1990;Sung & Lee, 2015). Therefore, recruiting scholars typically examine how employers can actively manage their company recruiting image. They especially assess efforts involving branding and impression management tactics (Cable & Judge, 2003;Yu, 2019); however, corporate images are also influenced by factors beyond the organization's control. Word-of-mouth (WOM), for instance, has challenged this norm of companies controlling their images through selectively curating information to convey in job advertisements and on corporate websites (e.g., Gregory et al., 2013). WOM is information that former and current employees and company affiliates informally share about their organizational experiences. Therefore, it cannot be regulated by organizations, as it is often disclosed privately to immediate social circles. Most job seekers, however, do not conveniently have immediate social contacts with first-hand knowledge of the culture and climate of targeted organizations. Instead, recent development in job search technology allows applicants to seek this WOM from third-party platforms (TPPs) for employee reviews. The popularity of these TPPs, such as Glassdoor and Indeed, has exploded with these sites now receiving between 60 and 250 million monthly visits from prospective candidates (Glassdoor, 2021;Indeed, 2020). Consequently, organizations' monopoly in sharing recruiting information has been negatively affected (McFarland & Ployhart, 2015) with their crafted employer recruiting messages being overwhelmed by the availability of TPP information (Chapman & Gödöllei, 2017). This trend underscores the importance of examining the properties of TPPs and the reach of their influence (Van Hoye & Lievens, 2007, 2009).

Few studies have explored the impact of review characteristics on recruiting outcomes (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020). Researchers have emphasized that empirical assessment of organic employee reviews on TPPs is particularly lacking (Pernkopf et al., 2021). Reading employee reviews is common practice during job search, where at least 70% of applicants retrieve this information to infer attributes about the organization (Kuadli, 2023), rather than utilizing a single review as examined in experimental inquiries (e.g., Van Hoye & Lievens, 2007). Accordingly, in response to calls for empirical research into this growing phenomenon (McFarland & Ployhart, 2015), we investigate the structure and effects of online reviews on potential job applicants' adoption of information conveyed about an organization. We, therefore, propose to i) examine the features and impact of third-party employee reviews' (TERs) components on their level of persuasion; ii) use machine learning to evaluate the topics discussed in TERs; and iii) assess the interaction effects of the extent of these topic discussions with sentiment. This research inquiry is among a few in the recruiting literature that assesses thousands of organic employer reviews across hundreds of companies, offering an in-depth review of what contributes to an influential job TER. To accomplish this goal, we examine 24,687 TERs across 451 companies. We will detail the use of automated web-scraping technology to collect thousands of TERs, and subject these to machine learning text analysis for interpretation.

We first describe the metric we use to evaluate the persuasiveness of different review components on TPPs (i.e., their level of helpfulness). Then we categorize TER properties as central or peripheral before discussing how certain features, such as sentiment, differentially influence specific outcomes, and whether organizational image and employee status moderate the strength of that relationship. Finally, we determine common TER topics discussed through machine learning text analysis and their relation to helpfulness ratings.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: TERs are typically accompanied with helpfulness ratings which indicate to readers how useful and influential a review is (Filieri, 2015;Zhu et al., 2014). Marketing scholars, (e.g., Risselada et al., 2018;Zhu et al., 2014) consider reviews helpful if the information shared is relevant (i.e., applicable in evaluating the product/service/experience) and diagnostic (i.e., assist the consumer in familiarizing themselves with a product which enables remote assessment of its quality and performance; Filieri, 2015). These factors contribute to how helpful the review is in facilitating information processing (Zhu et al., 2014), which increases the likelihood that this information will be adopted for evaluation and decision-making (Filieri, 2015;Sussman & Siegal, 2003). Helpfulness votes do not necessarily indicate whether candidates are ultimately persuaded by the review, but show what information is useful for evaluations. For example, an applicant may be convinced that an organization stunts career advancement through the reviews they have examined but may choose to accept an offer from this company as other factors might be more important to them.

The marketing literature provides some clues about how TERs might be evaluated by job seekers, however, we believe the implications surrounding job choice decisions warrant separate investigation. Collectively, the Elaboration Likelihood Model (ELM) and Subjective Value Maximization posit that one's involvement in information processing increases when the issue is personally relevant and has implications for their social presentation (Falk & Scholz, 2018;Howe & Krosnick, 2017). Therefore, highly involved individuals process information more deeply than those less involved (Petty & Wegener, 1998) as demonstrated in the recruiting literature (Jones et al., 2006). In general, job seekers are highly motivated to evaluate organizational reviews, compared to customers evaluating product reviews, as the consequences are greater for employment which often determines people's quality of life (Breaugh, 2013;Kruglanski, 1990;Pernkopf et al., 2021). For organizational reviews, we can infer from previous research that a motivated applicant will comprehensively examine employee narratives by evaluating both peripheral (e.g., review sentiment and consensus ratings) and central (i. e., message content) cues; thus, they would consider diverse cues throughout their decision-making (SanJosé-Cabezudo et al., 2009). The ELM articulates that peripheral cues are extraneous information people consciously or unconsciously share that influences others' perceptions of, and persuasion by, the message conveyed (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986). Examples of peripheral cues are attitudes embedded in the message, and perceptions of others' endorsement of the message (i.e., social consensus). These are peripheral, as attitudes and social consensus are typically external to the messages' validity and relevance, but can particularly influence those with high cognitive load, and lower motivation (Jones et al., 2006). On the contrary, central cues are those directly embedded in the content of the message, such as the topic discussed, accuracy of the argument and number of points shared (Petty & Wegener, 1998). Those with higher motivation, and lower cognitive load, tend to exert additional effort in analyzing a message's central information. We argue that because of the criticality of job choice, prospective applicants will collectively process both peripheral and central cues, contrary to typical product choice; therefore, this study offers incremental information beyond what has been previously investigated in the marketing literature. Specifically, we will investigate the unique influence of peripheral and central cues in context of employee reviews, to theoretically contribute to the recruiting discourse that has primarily focused on singular reviews. Prior experimental studies in the recruiting field (e.g., Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020) have typically assessed the characteristics of one review, and how that influences people's organizational perceptions. In the current study, we utilize field data to examine a broader sample of positive and negative reviews, all of which are available to the reader. As a result, we anticipate further nuance in our current study's findings.

Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Part of what contributes to the persuasiveness of TERs is the review's sentiment, which is indexed by the reviewer's rating that accompanies their experiences on the TPPs (Ghasemaghaei et al., 2018). Each reviewer rates their overall satisfaction with an organization on a scale of 1 (poor/negative) to 5 (excellent/positive). Negative reviews are typically construed as less ambiguous and more diagnostic (Cao et al., 2011;Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Filieri, 2015;Filieri et al., 2018Filieri et al., , 2019;;Kusumasondjaja et al., 2012;Lo & Yao, 2019;Stockman et al., 2020;Vaish et al., 2008;Wang et al., 2019), aligning with the negativity bias which outlines an innate tendency for people to process and be more persuaded by negative information (Vaish et al., 2008). Thus, potential candidates may appraise negative reviews as shielding them from poor personal and social consequences associated with the organization (Falk & Scholz, 2018); this is consistent with findings in the marketing literature which demonstrate that negative online customer reviews were considered more helpful or influential (Cao, Duan, & Gan, 2011;Filieri, 2015;Filieri et al., 2018Filieri et al., , 2019;;Kusumasondjaja et al., 2012;Lo & Yao, 2019;Vaish et al., 2008;Wang et al., 2019). Specifically, Kusumasondjaja et al. (2012) and Lo and Yao (2019) found that negative product information is perceived as more credible, and as a result attracts higher helpfulness votes; but positive product reviews elicit higher initial trust among readers (Kusumasondjaja et al., 2012). Given the added importance of securing employment, versus purchasing a product, we expect this increased scrutiny to elicit a similar but more pronounced pattern for employee reviewers.
Hypothesis 1. Prospective applicants will perceive negative reviews as more helpful than positive reviews.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Review sentiment, which is considered tangential to the information conveyed, may also interact with other peripheral cues, such as the characteristics of the review's content, as implied in the ELM (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986). Prior research, which evaluated the content within reviews, focused on exploratorily capturing the subject matter discussed (e.g., Dabirian et al., 2016;Sainju et al., 2021). This study extends this approach by investigating the extent to which the topic is addressed in the review, further layering the theoretical discourse in the literature. For instance, in assessing whether a sentiment is valid, applicants may evaluate the number of topics and the extent of a particular topic's discussion to contextualize the reviewer's attitude. For example, the perceived helpfulness of a review which thoroughly describes negative organizational experiences may be perceived more or less helpful compared to another reviewer who discusses this same experience but to a lesser degree. This elaboration is referred to as topic proportion, which describes the extent to which a reviewer discusses a specific topic within the review. For instance, a reviewer may describe their organization's leadership and co-worker relationships, however they could discuss these topics in varying degrees where leadership may dominate 70% of their review (i.e., topic proportion equates to .70). Ultimately, we expect that when negative information is highlighted and further elaborated in the discussion of a particular topic, it will receive higher traction than its discussion in smaller proportions. Echoing the previous arguments made, we attribute this pattern to a predominantly negative account of a topic being easier to evaluate and diagnose (Filieri, 2015).
Hypothesis 2. Higher topic proportions associated with negative sentiments are perceived as more helpful than smaller topic proportions associated with negative sentiments.

paragraphs: People evaluate the consensus in others' perceptions in determining their own attitudes. This constitutes another peripheral cue available on TPPs. This tendency is consistent with arguments proposed both by the dual processing model (i.e., the tendency for people to assess normative and informative cues; Deutsch & Gerard, 1955) and the subjective value maximization framework (Falk & Scholz, 2018). Thus, prospective candidates consider the overall aggregate rating of the organization, as this contextualizes how they process subsequent individual reviews about the company. This overall rating is an index of corporate image, which is conceptualized as the aggregate perceptions that stakeholders have about a company, including the attitudes, emotions, and/or thoughts that consumers and prospective applicants associate with an organization (Cian & Cervai, 2014), moderating the effectiveness of attracting and retaining applicants (Tsai & Yang, 2010;Yu, 2019).

On TPPs, applicants are provided with the average of all reviewers' ratings of a company in addition to the rating of any particular review they are reading. These consensus ratings may influence the effectiveness of individual positive or negative reviews (Stockman et al., 2020). Previous studies in the marketing and recruiting literature have demonstrated that a strong aggregate corporate image is more influential than a few negative opinions (e.g., Stockman et al., 2020). These patterns are more evident when the overall company ratings are unanimously positive or negative (i.e., either an extremely high or low rating), as readers can easily derive the collective opinion. With mixed sentiments (i.e., indexed by a mid-point rating), however, jobseekers may rely more on individual reviews, especially negative ones. Aligned with these arguments, Stockman et al. (2020) demonstrated that lower ranked organizations experienced declining applicant attraction in response to negative reviews. Conversely, companies with higher overall ratings were unchallenged by negative reviews. Hence, we expect that organizational ratings will depict a buffering effect, where negative reviews for highly rated companies will be considered less helpful due to their lack of credibility. Hypothesis 3. Negative reviews for high rated organizations are perceived as less helpful than those of low rated organizations.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Another peripheral cue that influences how applicants' process information is the source of the message, as people may use this to infer the credibility of the reviewer (Filieri et al., 2018(Filieri et al., , 2019)), affecting prospective candidates' decision-making (Van Hoye & Lievens, 2009). Particularly on TPPs, reviewer credibility is likely influenced by their employment status, including whether they are a current or former employee. For instance, Pernkopf et al. (2021) demonstrated through focus groups that participants perceived negative reviews from former employees as potentially biased due to a possible unsatisfactory layoff/resignment. Alternatively, Saini and Jawahar (2019) found that current and former employees have differential experiences (Sainju et al., 2021) that influence their company recommendations. They argue that because former employees have lower stakes in the organization, they are likely more honest and transparent in their accounts whereas current employees are likely to recommend their companies (Saini & Jawahar, 2019). This implies that readers with this mindset would be more persuaded by reviews from former employees. Given the conflicting findings from the qualitative and quantitative approaches, it is difficult to conclusively hypothesize a specific relationship, which is why we pose a research question.

paragraphs: As illustrated in previous studies in the marketing domain, certain peripheral components of an online review may be relatively more influential than others. In a meta-analysis of factors that contribute to a helpful consumer product review, Wang et al. (2019) found that the quality of information shared was most critical in facilitating decision making, followed by the sentiment and age of the review. Source credibility was found to be least helpful to product consumers. Similarly, for positive employee reviews, Evertz et al. (2019) demonstrated the importance of argument quality and source credibility in their effects on organizational attraction. It is curious how readers/prospective applicants would perceive these factors in an organizational review context. Like the studies conducted in the consumer literature, we also anticipate that certain components of employee reviews will be more influential. However, as this has not been previously assessed, it is premature to argue which specific peripheral factors would be most influential for prospective applicants. Considering the possibility that readers would differentially process employee reviews as opposed to consumer experiences, we instead raise the question of how TER components will compare in terms of their influence on helpfulness ratings.

paragraphs: 2.6. Review content Cao et al. (2011) illustrated, through their use of text mining techniques, that the semantic/central aspects of reviews are also critical in influencing helpfulness ratings. Previous research has illustrated how specific topics discussed throughout employee reviews provide insight into job satisfaction factors, and their implications for turnover (Jung & Suh, 2019;Lee & Kang, 2017;Luo et al., 2016;Sainju et al., 2021;Stamolampros et al., 2019) and corporate performance (Luo et al., 2016). The topics that were extracted across these studies typically concerned management (sometimes discussed in conjunction with leadership or employee well-being), work life balance, compensation, organizational culture, and career advancement (Dabirian et al., 2016;Jung & Suh, 2019;Lee & Kang, 2017;Luo et al., 2016;Sainju et al., 2021;Stamolampros et al., 2019). These studies provide insight into the topics we expect to emerge in the context of employee experiences. However, considering that the text mining of employee reviews is new to the recruiting literature, there may be additional topics not captured in prior research, especially with the limited samples of companies examined across specific studies. For instance, Dabirian et al. (2016) effectively outlined seven value propositions uncovered in employee reviews across 20 companies. Moreover, whereas Suen et al. (2020) analyzed at least 1 million reviews, they only assessed the keywords of pros and cons expressed across employee reviews. The current study expands this analysis to multiple reviews of 451 companies with the intention to validate and extend prior studies' findings. Further, with most of this research predominantly from the Computer Science domain, we hope to further contextualize these themes against theoretical frameworks based in recruitment to improve our understanding of what factors employees highlight and their influence on applicants. Through these lenses, we raise the question of what topics are typically discussed on employee review platforms.

paragraphs: Overall, our large-scale assessment of employee TERs serves to better understand the extent to which different components of a review influence someone's appraisals. Thus, we examined the effects of the sentiment, overall rating of the organization, the type of reviewer (former or current employee) on helpfulness ratings, the topics discussed and topic proportion (Fig. 1). An automated text analysis using Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA), with Structural Topic Modeling (STM) was used to determine the themes/topics discussed in the TERs.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: With permission from Glassdoor, we extracted and examined the peripheral and central cues of 24,686 employee reviews from their platform. Of these reviews, we assessed the peripheral cues of 22,523 reviews across 451 companies' profiles, with approximately 50 reviews per organization. Glassdoor reports that readers form an impression of the organization after reading approximately four reviews (Glassdoor, 2021), illustrating that scraping the most recent 50 reviews posted for companies likely reflects the information that prospective applicants would interact with. Companies with no helpfulness ratings for any of their reviews were omitted from the dataset. Further, reviews with extreme helpfulness votes were omitted from the dataset (Hair et al., 2010), as these were vastly distinct from the typical number of helpfulness votes given per review. After the omission of companies with no helpfulness votes, and extreme reviews, helpfulness ratings ranged from 0 to 18 per review. 49.9% percent of the Glassdoor reviews in this sample were provided by current employees where the average organizational rating was 3.55 (SD = .43). Reviews had a mean individual rating of 3.54 (SD = 1.30) and were found to be deviating from the aggregate organizational rating by 1.03 (SD = .73) points on average.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Octoparse, an open-source automated web-scraping tool, was used to extract data from companies' Glassdoor profiles. We evaluated the peripheral cues of these reviews, by assessing their correlations with helpfulness ratings, before examining the effects of the TER components on helpfulness ratings in a relative weights analysis. We then evaluated employee status and organizational ratings as moderators of the relationship between review sentiment and helpfulness ratings using PRO-CESS macro in SPSS developed by Hayes (2022). PROCESS macro is a widely used software that can be integrated with SPSS to efficiently and accurately run moderation and mediation paths or assess more complex structural models (see Hayes, 2022).

Further, we examined the hierarchical nature of the data, and whether this influenced the study's findings. As these reviews are clustered around specific companies, these organizations may differentially influence the helpfulness of these reviews. We first examined the unconditional model to determine the intraclass coefficient, which indicates the extent to which individual companies explain variance in review helpfulness. After, we specified companies as the level two clustering variable and included employee status (a dichotomous variable), individual ratings as level one variables, where individual ratings were group-mean centered. We also included grand mean centered overall organizational ratings as a level two predictor in the model. This analysis serves to further validate the study's findings by demonstrating consistency in results after accounting for the dataset's multilevel nature, and further mitigate overestimating our effects.

In examining the central cues, we combined the pros and cons for each review into one document for each reviewer, to increase the breadth and focus of the analyses. The data underwent preliminary analyses, before being examined in moderation and relative weights analyses. The reviews were then examined in R (programming language for statistical analyses) to determine the type of topics discussed, and to extract the document-topic proportions for further analyses (tidyverse, quanteda and stm packages were used; R Core Team, 2013). The TERs' readability (i.e., the ease of reading a chunk of text), word and character counts were first evaluated, as well as the word frequency plots. The TERs were converted to lowercase, and were stemmed, and trimmed.

We then determined the number of topics appropriate for the entire dataset. This involved running several series of topic models to assess their semantic coherency (i.e., interpretability) and held-out likelihood (i.e., predictive capacity). These metrics were used to compare the quality of these topic models, to then determine how many topics seem to best represent the discussions observed in the dataset. Once there was consensus on the topic-model that was most appropriate for the data, the corpus was submitted to a structural topic modeling analysis where employee status was entered as a covariate. The topics provided from the overall topic-model analyses were then interpreted using the words that had the highest probability of belonging to that topic (i.e., probability words) and words that were frequent and exclusive to that topic (i. e., FREX words). We also examined the reviews associated with the words represented in each topic to evaluate the context in which the words were used, to avoid misrepresenting the TERs. Once the individual topics were labelled, we assessed their level of interrater agreement with three psychometrically trained graduate students in Industrial/Organizational Psychology. These topics were then submitted to a hierarchical regression analysis, to assess the effects of their interaction with review sentiment on helpfulness ratings. We further verified the reviews' sentiments, by conducting a sentiment analysis on the reviews using the Afinn (Nielsen, 2011), Bing (Bing, 2010) and Syuzhen (Naldi, 2019) dictionaries. We found small to moderate positive correlations between these indices and individual ratings, providing some validation that dictionary indices are related to individual ratings. However, as we expected, the dictionaries had weaker correlations with helpfulness votes than direct proximal measures represented by the individual ratings (see supplemental online materials for full correlation matrix). This is likely due to readers requiring greater attention and motivation to interpret review sentiment via word choice than simply noting the score provided in the review. Accordingly, in order to manage the scope of the study, we focus here on the use of individual ratings. The datasets and scripts used in this study can also be accessed through our OSF [https://osf.io/q6gup/?view_only=97ccffc5737c4b3ea59030dd e1cd542f].

paragraphs: Topic Modeling is used to automate retrieving and classifying words (similar to codes in thematic analysis) used in chunks of text, into topics (commensurate to themes in thematic analysis, or latent factors in more traditional empirical analyses; Banks et al., 2018;Baumer et al., 2017;Doldor et al., 2019;Li et al., 2019). LDA is the most prevalent topic modeling approach in machine learning (Banks et al., 2018). LDA utilizes a "bag-of-words" approach which involves reducing the documents to terms and removing stop words/qualifiers that do not substantively contribute to the meaning of the text (e.g., "the", "a"). Words are stemmed and/or lemmatized (i.e., they have been reduced to their "base" or a simpler form; Banks et al., 2018;McKenny et al., 2018). For example, the stem "cultur" could be interpreted as "culture" and/or "cultural") to mitigate analytical redundancies, thus improving the quality of topic extraction (Banks et al., 2018). After documents are reduced to words, each word is assigned probabilities for belonging to each topic, and clusters of words are identified through their probabilities and co-occurrences with each other. It is this product that we would use to interpret the output of higher order topics or themes. The influence of covariates (e.g., employee status) on topics discussed was further assessed through structural topic modeling (see Banks et al., 2018 for further information about LDA).

Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Hypothesis 1, which proposed that negative sentiments (as indexed by lower individual ratings) would attract higher helpfulness ratings, was supported with a moderate negative association (r = -.33, p < .001). The remaining correlations between the TER components, and their descriptive statistics can be found in Table 1. As shown in Fig. 1, we then examined other peripheral cues, specifically overall company ratings and employee status, as moderators of the relationship between review sentiment and helpfulness ratings (Table 2). We predicted that company's aggregate ratings (i.e., the overall company rating on Glassdoor) would buffer the impact of negative reviews on helpfulness ratings. Overall, this model significantly explained 12% (p < .001) of variance in helpfulness ratings, with a significant interaction term between organizational ratings and review sentiment (R2change = .001, p < .001 As shown in Fig. 2, despite the low R 2 change for the interaction, negative reviews were least impactful on helpfulness ratings when overall company ratings were highest (β = -.34, SE = .017, p < .001), compared to when moderate (β = -.45, SE = .01, p < .001) and low (β = -.53, SE = .01, p < .001), thus, illustrating a partial buffering effect supporting Hypothesis 3.

Further, research questions 1a and 1b raised the questions of how employee status (0 = current employee, 1 = former employee) would be associated with helpfulness ratings and interact with review sentiment on helpfulness votes. The main effect revealed that former employees' reviews were generally considered more helpful than reviews provided by current employees. Overall, the model with employee status, review sentiment and an interaction term significantly moderated the relationship between review sentiment and helpfulness (R2 = .11, p < .001), with an interaction between review sentiment and employee status (R2 change = .003, p < .001). These results further demonstrated that negative reviews were especially influential on helpfulness votes from former employers (β = -.56, p < .001) than current employees (β = -.38, p < .001). As reviews increase in their positive sentiments, they were considered more helpful when shared by current employees.

Finally for peripheral cues, Research Question 2 raised the question of which peripheral component of a review would be more influential on helpfulness ratings. We ran an OLS multiple regression which showed indicators of suppression effects; the effect of employee status contradicted the direction of effects in the correlational and moderation analyses, as well as the regression analysis where employee status was the sole predictor. 1 To mitigate issues with suppression and potential multicollinearity, we instead ran a relative weights analysis (Table 3). Essentially, a relative weights analysis decomposes the variance accounted for by the regression model, into orthogonal weights that more precisely capture the proportional contribution of each of the predictors (see Tonidandel & LeBreton, 2011). The relative weights analysis included review sentiment, employee status and overall company ratings where review sentiment (93.96%) exhibited the strongest influence, followed by overall ratings (4.81%) and employee status (1.23%).

paragraphs: Assessing the unconditional model revealed that the 451 companies represented in the dataset explained 28.32% of variance in review helpfulness (Wald Z = 14.31, p < .001). As this is substantial variance explained, we assessed companies as our clustering variable in our multilevel analysis. Specifically, the model, which included group-mean centered individual ratings and grand-mean centered organizational ratings as covariates and employee status as a factor (-2 log likelihood = 72514.92, df = 11), significantly improved prediction over the unconditional model (-2 log likelihood = 87474.38, df = 3). Slopes of all predictors were statistically significant for group-centered individual ratings (t (464.48) = -15.93, p < .001), grand-centered organizational ratings (t (70.56) = -5.14, p < .001) and employee status (t (20280.02) = 4.52, p < .001). Controlling for employee status and organizational ratings, individual ratings significantly predicted an approximately .41 decline in helpfulness ratings (SE = .03). This was followed by organizational ratings, which predicted a .30 decrease in helpfulness ratings,

sections.header_norm: search for k number of topics
paragraphs: To determine the number of topics appropriate for this dataset (i.e., the number of issues discussed across reviews), we ran an extensive series of possible topic models, in increments of 5, that ranged from 5 to 65 topics, upon recommendation from the R Core Team (2013) given the scope of this dataset. These topic models were evaluated for their overall semantic coherence (i.e., how interpretable the topics were) and held-out likelihood (i.e., the predictive capacity for the topic models), which are the main components to evaluate for topic model quality (Banks et al., 2018). Based on these metrics, the 20-topic model demonstrated a balance between semantic coherence and held-out likelihood; however, upon closer observation, the topics within the model were less interpretable than the 25-topic model. We found that the largest set of topic models were too fine-grained to find coherency, and the smaller topic models lacked information shown present in those that were more extensive. Therefore, we focused our analyses on the topics that were in the mid-range (i.e., the 15, 20, 25 and 30 topic models) where the 25-topic model displayed the strongest predictive capacity. Though the 25-topic model had a lower semantic coherence than the 15 and 20 topic models, we could more effectively interpret these topics than the other models in the analyses. Therefore, we selected the 25-topic model.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Once the number of topics were decided and the topic modelling analyses were conducted, we assessed the content validity ratio for the topics' labels. This was done through a matching exercise with three Industrial-Organizational Psychology graduate students. This activity was structured with two columns where column one comprised the topic labels, and column two consisted of the groups of stemmed terms (both high probability and FREX words) that are affiliated with a specific label. We randomized the order of both the labels and grouped terms, then participants were asked to indicate which topic label they believed should be associated with each group of terms (see OSF: https://osf. io/q6gup/?view_only=97ccffc5737c4b3ea59030dde1cd542f for exercise used). Fleiss' Kappa was .778 (p < .001), indicating better than chance agreement, where one rater diverged in agreement on the topic labels for Growth Opportunities, Work Shifts/Hours and Vacation and Overtime. The raters overall proposed that two topics seemed to considerably overlap with each other (Company Culture/Climate and Climate and Pace), and as a result, we combined these topics' proportions to create a single topic.

representative reviews for each topic informed our labelling and interpretation of topics (Banks et al., 2018). Because the top nine topics for this model were the most interpretable, and the remaining were difficult to decipher, we restricted evaluation to the most coherent topics. Further, the most prevalent themes were Company Culture/Climate, Work-Life Balance and Compensation and Benefits. In answering Research Question 3, the topics extracted from the STM analysis are described in Table 4. Upon examining the reviews that best represented each topic, we noticed several overlaps in the themes discussed within that review. For instance, though review A may present the highest probability of belonging to topic 1, the issues discussed in review A are diverse enough to apply moderately well to the other topics. The nature of the STM is that all documents are assigned varying probabilities for all topics, however, we found that the reviews were varied with respect to the issues discussed. This is likely due to Glassdoor instructions priming reviewers to discuss a variety of topics. We, however, found that the representative reviews loaded better for their respective topics, than when examined in the 15, 20 and 30 topic models.

Topic Clusters. The nine topics identified (Table 4) seem to generally aggregate around two higher order categories -Work Environment (i.e., Company Culture/Climate, Work Life Balance, Management and Employee Well-being, Leadership Quality and Growth Opportunities) and Company Offerings (i.e., Compensation and Benefits, Training and Support, Work Shifts/Hours and Vacation and Overtime). Work Environment concerns descriptions of the nature of the workplacehow employees experience the company's climate, and the quality of treatment and direction of management/leadership that contribute to the company's overall ambiance. Company Offerings address topics concerning the provisions that the organization offers to incumbents, whether through opportunities to earn more (for example, via overtime or hours) and/or enjoy tangible/intangible benefits (for example, via benefits, vacation, or socioemotional support).

Topic Proportions' Interactions with Sentiment. Review sentiments were entered in the first step of the hierarchical regression, the document-topic proportions in the second step, and the interaction terms between review sentiment and the proportions in the third step, with helpfulness ratings as the outcome in this model. Considering that previous analyses showed that review sentiments were the strongest predictors of helpfulness ratings, it was important that we controlled for the effects of this variable first. As shown in Table 5 for review sentiment and topics, the first model (R = .11, adjusted R 2 = .01, SE = 6.61, F change = 296.50, p < .001) with only review sentiment and the second model with interactions (R = .21, adjusted R 2 = .04, SE = 6.50, F change = 14.77, p < .001) predict a significant change in the variance of helpfulness ratings, demonstrating that the sentiments associated with the topic proportions contextualize their influence on review helpfulness. The interactions were significant, except for those with Training and Support, Management and Employee Wellbeing and Compensation and Benefits. For these topics, it appears that review sentiments do not seem to affect their influence on helpfulness ratings. In general, negative discussions within a topic received the highest helpfulness ratings, but as topic proportions increased, helpfulness ratings decreased substantially for the topic's reviews that were negatively discussed. For instance, Work Life Balance (as shown in Fig. 3) was discussed in varying proportionssome reviewers addressed this organizational attribute in low, moderate, and high prevalence in their reviews (i.e., different proportions). When Work Life Balance was discussed with a negative sentiment it was regarded as more helpful than moderate and positive sentiments. However, as the proportion of conversation regarding Work Life Balance increased along with negative sentiments, helpfulness ratings declined. Specifically, low topic proportions associated with negative sentiments were considered more helpful, than high topic proportions with negative sentiments, which fail to support Hypothesis 2. We had hypothesized that an increase in the negative discussion of a topic would have attracted higher helpfulness ratings but found the reverse. Conversely, positive sentiments within TERs for the topics' document-topic proportions did not show changes in their trends as topic proportion increased. Another notable trend is that Leadership Quality was the only topic that depicted a positive trend, where topic proportions were associated with an increase in helpfulness ratings; and this was more prevalent for negative discussions for this topic.

For instance, though review A may present the highest probability of belonging to topic 1, the issues discussed in review A are diverse enough to apply moderately well to the other topics. The nature of the STM is that all documents are assigned varying probabilities for all topics, however, we found that the reviews were varied with respect to the issues discussed. This is likely due to Glassdoor instructions priming reviewers to discuss a variety of topics. We, however, found that the representative reviews loaded better for their respective topics, than when examined in the 15, 20 and 30 topic models.

Topic Clusters. The nine topics identified (Table 4) seem to generally aggregate around two higher order categories -Work Environment (i.e., Company Culture/Climate, Work Life Balance, Management and Employee Well-being, Leadership Quality and Growth Opportunities) and Company Offerings (i.e., Compensation and Benefits, Training and Support, Work Shifts/Hours and Vacation and Overtime). Work Environment concerns descriptions of the nature of the workplacehow employees experience the company's climate, and the quality of treatment and direction of management/leadership that contribute to the company's overall ambiance. Company Offerings address topics concerning the provisions that the organization offers to incumbents, whether through opportunities to earn more (for example, via overtime or hours) and/or enjoy tangible/intangible benefits (for example, via benefits, vacation, or socioemotional support).

Topic Proportions' Interactions with Sentiment. Review sentiments were entered in the first step of the hierarchical regression, the document-topic proportions in the second step, and the interaction terms between review sentiment and the proportions in the third step, with helpfulness ratings as the outcome in this model. Considering that previous analyses showed that review sentiments were the strongest predictors of helpfulness ratings, it was important that we controlled for the effects of this variable first. As shown in Table 5 for review sentiment and topics, the first model (R = .11, adjusted R 2 = .01, SE = 6.61, F change = 296.50, p < .001) with only review sentiment and the second model with interactions (R = .21, adjusted R 2 = .04, SE = 6.50, F change = 14.77, p < .001) predict a significant change in the variance of helpfulness ratings, demonstrating that the sentiments associated with the topic proportions contextualize their influence on review helpfulness. The interactions were significant, except for those with Training and Support, Management and Employee Wellbeing and Compensation and Benefits. For these topics, it appears that review sentiments do not seem to affect their influence on helpfulness ratings. In general, negative discussions within a topic received the highest helpfulness ratings, but as topic proportions increased, helpfulness ratings decreased substantially for the topic's reviews that were negatively discussed. For instance, Work Life Balance (as shown in Fig. 3) was discussed in varying proportionssome reviewers addressed this organizational attribute in low, moderate, and high prevalence in their reviews (i.e., different proportions). When Work Life Balance was discussed with a negative sentiment it was regarded as more helpful than moderate and positive sentiments. However, as the proportion of conversation regarding Work Life Balance increased along with negative sentiments, helpfulness ratings declined. Specifically, low topic proportions associated with negative sentiments were considered more helpful, than high topic proportions with negative sentiments, which fail to support Hypothesis 2. We had hypothesized that an increase in the negative discussion of a topic would have attracted higher helpfulness ratings but found the reverse. Conversely, positive sentiments within TERs for the topics' document-topic proportions did not show changes in their trends as topic proportion increased. Another notable trend is that Leadership Quality was the only topic that depicted a positive trend, where topic proportions were associated with an increase in helpfulness ratings; and this was more prevalent for negative discussions for this topic.

sections.header_raw: Post-Hoc Analyses of review length and proportion.
sections.header_norm: post hoc analyses of review length and proportion
paragraphs: It is possible that the length of the review (i.e., word count) potentially influences how these topic proportions affect helpfulness ratingsspecifically, we examine whether lengthy but narrowed (i.e., predominantly discussing one or a few topics) reviews are more helpful than lengthy but varied (i.

e., discussing several topics) reviews. We conducted post-hoc analyses to address these questions by taking the highest topic proportion loading for each document to represent the extent to which it captured a variety of topics (i.e., Max Proportion: the document's level of focus). Each document is assigned a proportion value for each topic, which all sum to 1. In other words, a review is assessed on the extent to which it captures each topic; if a review's highest topic loading was .7, and this was associated with Company Culture/Climate, it indicates that 70% of the document/review contained information that addressed this topic. It further highlights that the review was more focused on one or a few topics. Conversely, if the highest topic loading for another review was .3, it means that the review was varied in the type of information it contains. We, therefore, assessed if these effects would contextualize our earlier findings.

We regressed helpfulness ratings on the highest proportions for each document (labelled as Max Proportion), as well as their interactions with review length (or word count; Fig. 4 in Appendix A). The findings revealed that reviews that were more narrowed in their analysis of a specific topic, received less helpfulness ratings (Table 5). And, while lengthier reviews were generally considered most helpful, long but varied documents attracted the highest helpfulness ratings (see Appendix A). The regression weights were strongest and negative for Max Proportion suggesting that how a topic is discussed (length and variety), is likely as important as what is discussed.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Despite the widescale impact of online TERs on applicant attraction and the far-reaching implications of candidates gaining insight into employees' experiences, the examination of TERs has been largely unaddressed in the literature (Pernkopf et al., 2021). In uncovering these effects, we evaluated reviews' sentiments through their individual ratings and their subsequent influence through helpfulness indictors, as well as how this relationship is moderated by overall company ratings, and the reviewers' employee statuses. After, we conducted the topic modelling analysis to retrieve the most prevalent themes discussed in the TERs; their proportions were examined for their interaction effects with review sentiment on helpfulness ratings.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Overall, we demonstrate the value of review sentiment, company ratings, employee status and topic proportions in understanding the nature of employee reviews and their differential influence. We illustrate employee review sentiment's persuasion in a field context, validating experimental studies that used recruiting-based variables as their criteria, and only assessed individual reviews (e.g., Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020;Van Hoye & Lievens, 2007). However, we further expand beyond negative and positive sentiments to outline the different issues that employees discuss across hundreds of companies. Researchers such as Dabirian et al. (2016) and Suen et al. (2020) also effectively assessed content across reviews. We contribute additional insights on the topics outlined and how the way these topics are discussed may limit their persuasiveness. Thus, we interject further nuance on the influence of review sentiment, accounting for message source, corporate branding and topic proportion. For instance, we show that while negative reviews are generally more helpful, they begin to lose their influence when the reviewer extensively elaborates their issues on a specific topic.

Specifically, we anticipated and found that negative reviews are considered more helpful than positive experiences which aligns with previous research on OCRs where negative reviews were used by consumers to inform their decision-making when purchasing consumer products and services (Filieri, 2015;Filieri et al., 2018Filieri et al., , 2019)). More broadly, it is consistent with the negativity bias (Vaish et al., 2008), as well as the subjective value maximization and ELM concepts, which argue that conveying personally relevant information increases another's elaboration of and persuasion by that message (Falk & Scholz, 2018;Howe & Krosnick, 2017;Petty & Cacioppo, 1986); further, people perceive negative information to be more protective of their wellbeing and thus, more credible (Cheung et al., 2009;Lo & Yao, 2019). Combined with the high-stakes nature of pursuing employment intentions with an organization (Breaugh, 2013), it is reasonable to conclude that more sceptical information receives higher instances of helpfulness ratings.

We then focused on whether an overall positive average rating from many reviewers would mitigate the negative impact of individual negative reviews. Our data demonstrated that the capacity for negative reviews to impact review helpfulness and potentially, information adoption, is partially lowered when the company conveys higher brand equity or image through their overall rating. These field results support the few studies in the recruiting literature that have assessed overall ratings' impact on negative information's effects on applicant attraction (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020). Further, this finding is consistent with research on the benefits of corporate image and its capacity to mitigate the effects of scandal and negative WOM (Allen et al., 2007;Filieri, 2015;Melian-Gonzalez & Bulchand-Gidumal, 2016;Yu, 2019). However, like previous research (e.g., Stockman et al., 2020), the current study illustrated that corporate image did not completely buffer the impact of negative reviews. Stockman et al. (2020) further outlined the roles of consensus among multiple sources, direct experience with the organization and confirmation of information as additional variables that further buffer or strengthen the effects of negative reviews. We further query additional factors that may minimize the influence of negative reviews. In our assessment of central cues, we discovered specific "ranting" cues or extended elaborations on a specific topic that potentially reduce the credibility of the review. Therefore, these could serve as potential factors that undermine reviews' effectiveness, which we propose should be thoroughly examined in further studies. Moreover, the patterns found in prior research with the effects of review characteristics on applicant attraction are similar to those we uncovered with helpfulness ratings. We argue that helpfulness votes are an indicator of some level of persuasion to adopt the information presented in the review, therefore it is possible that helpfulness votes and applicant attraction are conceptually linked. If this is empirically true, then it would indicate that helpfulness votes index the impact of reviews on important recruiting outcomes (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020), thus implying the effects of employee experiences on the quality and depth of applicant pools.

We also found that, according to our relative weights and multilevel analyses, review sentiment was the strongest predictor of helpfulness ratings followed by overall organizational ratings and employee status. Therefore, these results demonstrate that sentiment takes precedence in processing reviews among the peripheral cues examined in the current study. These patterns also overlap with some of the OCR components Filieri (2015) examined where, outside of information quality, customer consensus ratings had the highest effect on information diagnosticity (i. e., the extent to which assessing information is aided) followed by rank (i.e., overall product ranking) and source credibility (Wang et al., 2019). Thus, review sentiments were still notably influential for customer reviews, as it has also been in employee reviews (Filieri, 2015). However, given the gravity of job choice, as opposed to consumer choice, it is likely that applicants weigh central information or cues as equally, if not more, critical in learning more about the respective organization. This requires a rigorous and direct comparison in pinpointing the limitations of review sentiment, compared to central cues, and especially within the larger context of the type of review (i.e., employee versus consumer) under assessment. Therefore, we recommend that this is empirically assessed in further studies.

We further examined the central cues in reviews by extracting the topics employees discussed on TPPs and evaluating how these topics would interact with review sentiment in influencing helpfulness ratings.

Similar to previous studies that have extracted topics from employee TPPs to assess their overlap with job satisfaction indices and corporate performance (Sainju et al., 2021), our themes also largely addressed the Work Environment (i.e., topics related to Leadership, Growth Opportunities, Work Life Balance) and Company Offerings (i.e., topics related to corporate benefits and support provided). We also identified additional topics, when we compare these to prior studies' findings (e.g., Dabirian et al., 2016;Suen et al., 2020). For instance, we show employees' discussions around training and support, executive leadership and the importance of work flexibility (i.e., Vacation and Overtime). We further show that while management is crucial, it is often discussed alongside or as influencing employees' well-being. Thus, the findings of this study serve to enrich the literature on the issues that employees highlight in their reviews. Given that the overarching goal of our topic modelling analyses was to summarize and contextualize the core discussions on these platforms, we believe that the themes extracted appear to be consistent with the image (Beach, 1990;Lievens & Highhouse, 2004) and fit theories (Cable & Edwards, 2004) from the recruiting literature.

In the qualitative assessment of the two higher-order clusters identified in the text analyses, we liken these to the symbolic and instrumental images of organizations articulated in the image theory. As conceptualized by Beach (1990) and further expanded by Lievens and Highhouse (2003), the image theory proposes that persons evaluate the "objective" or "tangible" provisions (such as compensation and benefits; instrumental image), and the values and culture of the organization (i.e., symbolic image; Highhouse et al., 1999). As such, we propose that the instrumental image of a company is construed through the topics clustered around the Company Offerings theme identified, and that the Work Environment cluster provides valuable insight into the company's symbolic image. Considering that prospective applicants also assess their fit with organizations, it is expected that they use their overall impressions of the company's instrumental image to infer their need-supplies fit with the company. Need-supplies fit refers to the perceived alignment between what a company provides and the sufficiency of these provisions for the prospective applicant (Peltokorpi et al., 2019). Moreover, supplementary fit is concerned with the alignment of values (example: cultural, work and career growth values; Cable & Edwards, 2004); thus, in assessing the symbolic image of the company through the Work Environment cluster, one may be able to determine their level of supplementary fit.

We hypothesized that reviewers who engage in lengthier discussion about a specific topic would be more influential if they negatively frame that experience. However, our findings illustrated otherwise, where lengthier topic discussions were more effective if the negative sentiments were minimized. Thus, the general trend that was noticed across most of the topics' proportions that significantly interacted with review sentiment, was that there was a general decline in helpfulness ratings as these proportions increase. Therefore, people are less receptive to reviews that discuss fewer topics and only express disapproval in the few topics addressed. As such, negative information for a specific topic is more influential when the disapproving aspects are in a review covering more topics. Conveying excessive critical experiences may lead to perceptions of ranting about a narrow topic rather than demonstrating competence by discussing a range of information. Consistent with the ELM, message recipients peripherally process the number of arguments in a message to assess the credibility of the information shared (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986). For instance, a reader may specifically want to learn about the global leadership of the company; however, a review may instead largely focus on lower-level management which would not be considered helpful. Discussing a larger variety of topics increases the likelihood that readers may find a discussion point that is helpful to them in their decision-making (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986;Petty & Wegener, 1998). This is also consistent with subjective value maximization framework where the audience is more perceptive of and persuaded by information that is consequential to them (Falk & Scholz, 2018).

Within each topic, negative sentiments were associated with higher helpfulness ratings and moderate to positive sentiments showed little to no change in their trends as the topic's proportion increased. Though negative sentiments have previously shown to gain more traction, overly critical discussion of limited content in reviews detracts from these effects. Further, our results are somewhat consistent with previous arguments made in the marketing literature, where extremely negative reviews may be perceived as untrustworthy, and that factors such as review length, may improve their credibility (Filieri et al., 2018(Filieri et al., , 2019)). This is due to the possible perception that negative elaborations may be regarded as overly focused rants that are less balanced in their accounts. Considering the nascent nature of these findings, future research could explore the limited effects of sentiments on persuasion, and if elaboration on a specific attitude or topic could operate as a moderator.

We illustrate employee review sentiment's persuasion in a field context, validating experimental studies that used recruiting-based variables as their criteria, and only assessed individual reviews (e.g., Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020;Van Hoye & Lievens, 2007). However, we further expand beyond negative and positive sentiments to outline the different issues that employees discuss across hundreds of companies. Researchers such as Dabirian et al. (2016) and Suen et al. (2020) also effectively assessed content across reviews. We contribute additional insights on the topics outlined and how the way these topics are discussed may limit their persuasiveness. Thus, we interject further nuance on the influence of review sentiment, accounting for message source, corporate branding and topic proportion. For instance, we show that while negative reviews are generally more helpful, they begin to lose their influence when the reviewer extensively elaborates their issues on a specific topic.

Specifically, we anticipated and found that negative reviews are considered more helpful than positive experiences which aligns with previous research on OCRs where negative reviews were used by consumers to inform their decision-making when purchasing consumer products and services (Filieri, 2015;Filieri et al., 2018Filieri et al., , 2019)). More broadly, it is consistent with the negativity bias (Vaish et al., 2008), as well as the subjective value maximization and ELM concepts, which argue that conveying personally relevant information increases another's elaboration of and persuasion by that message (Falk & Scholz, 2018;Howe & Krosnick, 2017;Petty & Cacioppo, 1986); further, people perceive negative information to be more protective of their wellbeing and thus, more credible (Cheung et al., 2009;Lo & Yao, 2019). Combined with the high-stakes nature of pursuing employment intentions with an organization (Breaugh, 2013), it is reasonable to conclude that more sceptical information receives higher instances of helpfulness ratings.

We then focused on whether an overall positive average rating from many reviewers would mitigate the negative impact of individual negative reviews. Our data demonstrated that the capacity for negative reviews to impact review helpfulness and potentially, information adoption, is partially lowered when the company conveys higher brand equity or image through their overall rating. These field results support the few studies in the recruiting literature that have assessed overall ratings' impact on negative information's effects on applicant attraction (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020). Further, this finding is consistent with research on the benefits of corporate image and its capacity to mitigate the effects of scandal and negative WOM (Allen et al., 2007;Filieri, 2015;Melian-Gonzalez & Bulchand-Gidumal, 2016;Yu, 2019). However, like previous research (e.g., Stockman et al., 2020), the current study illustrated that corporate image did not completely buffer the impact of negative reviews. Stockman et al. (2020) further outlined the roles of consensus among multiple sources, direct experience with the organization and confirmation of information as additional variables that further buffer or strengthen the effects of negative reviews. We further query additional factors that may minimize the influence of negative reviews. In our assessment of central cues, we discovered specific "ranting" cues or extended elaborations on a specific topic that potentially reduce the credibility of the review. Therefore, these could serve as potential factors that undermine reviews' effectiveness, which we propose should be thoroughly examined in further studies. Moreover, the patterns found in prior research with the effects of review characteristics on applicant attraction are similar to those we uncovered with helpfulness ratings. We argue that helpfulness votes are an indicator of some level of persuasion to adopt the information presented in the review, therefore it is possible that helpfulness votes and applicant attraction are conceptually linked. If this is empirically true, then it would indicate that helpfulness votes index the impact of reviews on important recruiting outcomes (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Stockman et al., 2020), thus implying the effects of employee experiences on the quality and depth of applicant pools.

We also found that, according to our relative weights and multilevel analyses, review sentiment was the strongest predictor of helpfulness ratings followed by overall organizational ratings and employee status. Therefore, these results demonstrate that sentiment takes precedence in processing reviews among the peripheral cues examined in the current study. These patterns also overlap with some of the OCR components Filieri (2015) examined where, outside of information quality, customer consensus ratings had the highest effect on information diagnosticity (i. e., the extent to which assessing information is aided) followed by rank (i.e., overall product ranking) and source credibility (Wang et al., 2019). Thus, review sentiments were still notably influential for customer reviews, as it has also been in employee reviews (Filieri, 2015). However, given the gravity of job choice, as opposed to consumer choice, it is likely that applicants weigh central information or cues as equally, if not more, critical in learning more about the respective organization. This requires a rigorous and direct comparison in pinpointing the limitations of review sentiment, compared to central cues, and especially within the larger context of the type of review (i.e., employee versus consumer) under assessment. Therefore, we recommend that this is empirically assessed in further studies.

We further examined the central cues in reviews by extracting the topics employees discussed on TPPs and evaluating how these topics would interact with review sentiment in influencing helpfulness ratings.

Similar to previous studies that have extracted topics from employee TPPs to assess their overlap with job satisfaction indices and corporate performance (Sainju et al., 2021), our themes also largely addressed the Work Environment (i.e., topics related to Leadership, Growth Opportunities, Work Life Balance) and Company Offerings (i.e., topics related to corporate benefits and support provided). We also identified additional topics, when we compare these to prior studies' findings (e.g., Dabirian et al., 2016;Suen et al., 2020). For instance, we show employees' discussions around training and support, executive leadership and the importance of work flexibility (i.e., Vacation and Overtime). We further show that while management is crucial, it is often discussed alongside or as influencing employees' well-being. Thus, the findings of this study serve to enrich the literature on the issues that employees highlight in their reviews. Given that the overarching goal of our topic modelling analyses was to summarize and contextualize the core discussions on these platforms, we believe that the themes extracted appear to be consistent with the image (Beach, 1990;Lievens & Highhouse, 2004) and fit theories (Cable & Edwards, 2004) from the recruiting literature.

In the qualitative assessment of the two higher-order clusters identified in the text analyses, we liken these to the symbolic and instrumental images of organizations articulated in the image theory. As conceptualized by Beach (1990) and further expanded by Lievens and Highhouse (2003), the image theory proposes that persons evaluate the "objective" or "tangible" provisions (such as compensation and benefits; instrumental image), and the values and culture of the organization (i.e., symbolic image; Highhouse et al., 1999). As such, we propose that the instrumental image of a company is construed through the topics clustered around the Company Offerings theme identified, and that the Work Environment cluster provides valuable insight into the company's symbolic image. Considering that prospective applicants also assess their fit with organizations, it is expected that they use their overall impressions of the company's instrumental image to infer their need-supplies fit with the company. Need-supplies fit refers to the perceived alignment between what a company provides and the sufficiency of these provisions for the prospective applicant (Peltokorpi et al., 2019). Moreover, supplementary fit is concerned with the alignment of values (example: cultural, work and career growth values; Cable & Edwards, 2004); thus, in assessing the symbolic image of the company through the Work Environment cluster, one may be able to determine their level of supplementary fit.

We hypothesized that reviewers who engage in lengthier discussion about a specific topic would be more influential if they negatively frame that experience. However, our findings illustrated otherwise, where lengthier topic discussions were more effective if the negative sentiments were minimized. Thus, the general trend that was noticed across most of the topics' proportions that significantly interacted with review sentiment, was that there was a general decline in helpfulness ratings as these proportions increase. Therefore, people are less receptive to reviews that discuss fewer topics and only express disapproval in the few topics addressed. As such, negative information for a specific topic is more influential when the disapproving aspects are in a review covering more topics. Conveying excessive critical experiences may lead to perceptions of ranting about a narrow topic rather than demonstrating competence by discussing a range of information. Consistent with the ELM, message recipients peripherally process the number of arguments in a message to assess the credibility of the information shared (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986). For instance, a reader may specifically want to learn about the global leadership of the company; however, a review may instead largely focus on lower-level management which would not be considered helpful. Discussing a larger variety of topics increases the likelihood that readers may find a discussion point that is helpful to them in their decision-making (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986;Petty & Wegener, 1998). This is also consistent with subjective value maximization framework where the audience is more perceptive of and persuaded by information that is consequential to them (Falk & Scholz, 2018).

Within each topic, negative sentiments were associated with higher helpfulness ratings and moderate to positive sentiments showed little to no change in their trends as the topic's proportion increased. Though negative sentiments have previously shown to gain more traction, overly critical discussion of limited content in reviews detracts from these effects. Further, our results are somewhat consistent with previous arguments made in the marketing literature, where extremely negative reviews may be perceived as untrustworthy, and that factors such as review length, may improve their credibility (Filieri et al., 2018(Filieri et al., , 2019)). This is due to the possible perception that negative elaborations may be regarded as overly focused rants that are less balanced in their accounts. Considering the nascent nature of these findings, future research could explore the limited effects of sentiments on persuasion, and if elaboration on a specific attitude or topic could operate as a moderator.

sections.paper_id: Morgan and Chapman - 2024 - Examining the characteristics and effectiveness of online employee reviews
paragraphs: Considering that employee experiences that address several topics in a balanced but critical manner, are associated with higher helpfulness ratings, implies the kind of information that is likely to be adopted on these platforms (Filieri, 2015, ;Zhu et al., 2014). If companies neglect to address how their image is being portrayed through their overall ratings, this will facilitate the ease at which negative information is adopted and used for decision-making. Thus, it is important that organizations develop an aptitude for evaluating their employees' experiences in an intentional way and examine what leads to workers' need to publicize their narratives online. Neglecting to implement mechanisms that effectively support employee disclosure, eventually leads to a declining corporate image that renders efforts to protect against scandal and attract competent workers, futile (Chapman & Webster, 2006;Stockman et al., 2020). Another important practical takeaway from these findings is that former employees' reviews attracted higher helpfulness ratings than current employees, and this was even more pronounced when former employees were moderately to extremely negative. Considering that research from both the marketing and recruiting literatures have outlined potential organizational responses that can mitigate the effects of negative reviews (Carpentier & Van Hoye, 2021;Lee & Cranage, 2014), organizations that actively respond to these reviews on Glassdoor may find it more effective to prioritize those of former employees. Further, if applicable, organizations should arrange for Human Resource management to improve the mechanisms underlying employees' departure from the organization. It would be more effective to examine and address departing employee concerns as they exit the company, than having to respond to the fallout from former employees publicly broadcasting their negative experiences after their departure.

We previously discussed that, applicants who are more competitive, are indeed more sensitive to negative information about the organization (Chapman & Webster, 2006). Thus, recruiters/interviewers may need to consider ways in which they can initiate discussions about the specific issues highlighted on review platforms, to alleviate or clarify expectations or potential concerns. On the other hand, the topics we extracted illustrate the specific issues that reviewers tend to emphasize, and therefore can be used to centre discussions among organizational members to improve the internal climate.

Further, the insights that we uncovered on the characteristics of review content that are most influential, can be utilized by TPPs such as Glassdoor or Indeed to support with structuring reviews in a format that is most useful for jobseekers. Currently, the interface of employer TPPs offer "helpfulness ratings" as a filter option to organize reviews from most to least helpful. The current study further demonstrates the role of employee status, and the topics that applicants discuss. As a result, these TPPs can provide a dropdown list of topics that are often discussed on their platform and use text-retrieval software to locate reviews most likely addressing that topic. TPPs can also provide additional tips to reviewers on addressing multiple topics in their review to more effectively support others' job search process, with potential reward systems implemented as incentives.
